translation: Alif, Lam, Meem, Ra. These are the verses of the Book; and what has been revealed to you from your Lord is the truth, but most of the people do not believe
commentary:
الٓمٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

The surah opens with the mysterious letters Alif-Lam-Mim-Ra. These disconnected letters remind us that the Quran is composed of the same Arabic letters the Quraysh use daily, yet they cannot produce anything like it.

The name of this surah, Ar-Ra'd (Thunder), signals that warning is coming. Just as thunder announces a powerful storm, this surah carries stern warnings for those who reject the truth.

This opening ayah connects directly to the conclusion of the previous surah, Yusuf. Surah Yusuf ended by establishing that the Quran is not fabricated speech, but rather confirmation of previous revelation. Now Surah Ar-Ra'd continues that conversation: if you know it's not made up, then what is it? How does it provide guidance?

تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (Those are the miraculous signs of the Book) - Allah ﷻ describes these ayahs as miraculous signs. The word آيَـٰتُ means both verses and signs, reminding us that every verse of the Quran is itself a miracle.

وَٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ٱلْحَقُّ (And whatever has been sent down to you from your Master is the absolute truth) - This revelation has a clear source: your Master, your Rabb. When Allah ﷻ uses the word رَبِّكَ (your Rabb), He's emphasizing the personal relationship between the Prophet ﷺ and his Lord. This isn't just any truth; it comes from the One who created you, sustains you and guides you.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (However, most people will not believe) - Despite the clarity and authenticity of this revelation, Allah ﷻ tells us from the very beginning that most people won't accept it. This prepares the Messenger ﷺ for the difficult reality ahead. The surah begins with a tough tone, acknowledging that resistance to truth is the norm, not the exception.

translation: It is Allah who erected the heavens without pillars that you [can] see; then He established Himself above the Throne and made subject the sun and the moon, each running [its course] for a specified term. He arranges [each] matter; He details the signs that you may, of the meeting with your Lord, be certain
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى رَفَعَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا ۖ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ يَجْرِى لِأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ

After establishing that revelation is truth from Allah ﷻ, this ayah now describes the One from whom this revelation came. Where did this message originate? From the heavens. So Allah ﷻ draws our attention upward.

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى رَفَعَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا (Allah is the One who raised the skies without any pillars that you can see) - Sometimes in the Quran, Allah ﷻ simply says "He raised the heavens." Other times He adds this striking detail: without pillars. Why mention it here specifically? Because revelation was just discussed, and revelation comes from the sky. By describing how high and grand and unsupported the heavens are, Allah ﷻ is helping us appreciate how incredible revelation itself is. The road that revelation takes to reach you is magnificent beyond description.

ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ (Then He rose upon the Throne) - This describes Allah's supreme authority over all creation. His dominion is absolute and His command is executed throughout the universe.

وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ (And He subdued the sun and the moon) - The word سَخَّرَ means to domesticate something and put it to work, like we do with animals. Allah ﷻ has subdued these celestial bodies and put them to work for us.

كُلٌّ يَجْرِى لِأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى (Everything is flowing, moving until a given time) - Allah ﷻ gives us a description of the universe He created: the skies, the earth, the sun, the moon. Everything is in constant motion. In nature, nothing is truly still. Inside every cell, inside every atom, particles are moving. The universe is in a perpetual state of motion and transformation. This motion continues until a given time - until the Day when the trumpet will be blown and everything will become still.

This motion also implies change. Everything is moving, altering, transforming until an appointed end. When Allah ﷻ says يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ (He plans out every matter), He's telling us that all these changes, all these movements, every single step in this cosmic transformation is planned by Him.

يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ (He clarifies the signs so that you may become completely convinced of meeting with your Master) - Why is Allah ﷻ describing all this cosmic motion? Because changes are happening constantly in the universe, and changes are happening constantly in you. One change that's progressing inevitably is our movement toward death. If you realize everything in the universe is changing, including yourself, then you should realize that just as you have an end, the entire universe has an end. The motion of the universe itself is proof that we will meet Allah ﷻ.

translation: And it is He who spread the earth and placed therein firmly set mountains and rivers; and from all of the fruits He made therein two mates; He causes the night to cover the day. Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى مَدَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنْهَـٰرًا ۖ وَمِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ جَعَلَ فِيهَا زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۖ يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ

After describing the heavens, Allah ﷻ now brings our attention to the earth.

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى مَدَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ (And He is the One who stretched the earth) - What land seems more stretched than the desert? The audience hearing this in Makkah can easily visualize vast, expansive land.

وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ (And He placed in it firmly anchored mountains) - The word رَوَٰسِىَ describes mountains where the larger part sits beneath the earth's surface and only a smaller portion is visible above, like pegs holding the earth in place.

وَأَنْهَـٰرًا (And rivers) - Now we have a complete image: land, mountains protecting it, and rivers flowing through. If you have irrigated land protected by mountains, what naturally follows? Vegetation. Abundant fruit.

This imagery is particularly meaningful for the people of Makkah. Their city is surrounded by mountains and has a water supply (Zamzam), yet fruits don't grow from that land. Instead, fruits come to Makkah from everywhere else. People bring all kinds of produce to this safe city, fulfilling the prayer of Ibrahim (AS) that its people be provided with all varieties of fruit.

وَمِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ جَعَلَ فِيهَا زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ (From every fruit He placed in it two pairs) - The word زَوْجَيْنِ indicates pairs or types. Some scholars said this refers to male and female species of plants, as some plants contain both within themselves while others have separate genders. Others understood it as variations within the same fruit - some tasty, some not; some sweet, some bitter.

This is one of many contrasts that define Surah Ar-Ra'd. From a stylistic perspective, this surah is built on contrasts: night and day, sky and earth, believer and disbeliever, mountains and flat land, different types of fruit. You'll continue to see these contrasts throughout.

يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ (He covers the night with day) - Or this can be understood as He covers the day with night. The Arabic allows both readings. Another powerful contrast.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (Indeed, in all of that are multiple signs for people who deeply engage themselves in thought) - These aren't just agricultural observations. For people who truly reflect - يَتَفَكَّرُونَ - there are profound lessons here about revelation, guidance and human nature.

translation: And within the land are neighboring plots and gardens of grapevines and crops and palm trees, [growing] several from a root or otherwise, watered with one water; but We make some of them exceed others in [quality of] fruit. Indeed in that are signs for a people who reason
commentary:
وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُّتَجَـٰوِرَٰتٌ وَجَنَّـٰتٌ مِّنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ وَزَرْعٌ وَنَخِيلٌ صِنْوَانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوَانٍ يُسْقَىٰ بِمَآءٍ وَٰحِدٍ وَنُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَهَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِى ٱلْأُكُلِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ

وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُّتَجَـٰوِرَٰتٌ (And especially in the earth, there are portions of land right next to each other) - The word مُّتَجَـٰوِرَٰتٌ comes from the root meaning "neighbor." These are neighboring parcels of land, adjoining one another. Sometimes on a road you'll see trees standing in rows like an army, even though no one planted them that way. They naturally formed these patterns.

وَجَنَّـٰتٌ مِّنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ وَزَرْعٌ وَنَخِيلٌ (And gardens of grapes, all kinds of crops and palm trees) - Allah ﷻ is painting a detailed landscape of agricultural diversity.

صِنْوَانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوَانٍ (Some combined and some not combined) - This introduces a new word: صِنْوَانٌ. It describes date palms that stand firm on a single stem without branching much, or alternatively, palms made up of many branches coming from one thick stalk. The point is there are different varieties and structures of trees.

يُسْقَىٰ بِمَآءٍ وَٰحِدٍ (Yet all are given to drink from the same water) - Here's the profound observation that leads to deep reflection. All these different plants - the neighboring plots, the grape vines, the crops, the various palm trees - they're all drinking the same water from the same source.

But here's where the parallel becomes clear. Water in the Quran is consistently used as a metaphor for revelation. Water is pure and purifies everything else. What is revelation? Pure and purifying. Water comes from the sky and gives life to the earth. Revelation comes from the sky and gives life to hearts and societies. Water nourishes different kinds of plants. Revelation benefits different kinds of people.

Sometimes the same tree drinks identical water through its roots, distributing the same nutrients to all its fruits, yet some fruits come out sweet and others bitter. Some are nourishing, others are not. Why? Same household, same revelation, same message - one uncle believes, another uncle disbelieves. Same water, different results.

وَنُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَهَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِى ٱلْأُكُلِ (And We give preference to some over others in taste and texture) - Allah ﷻ makes some plants more beneficial than others. Some plants become a source of life for other plants. Trees grow tall and their shade protects smaller plants that couldn't survive the intensity of direct sunlight. The messengers are like those trees - they take all the heat and provide shade for others. They have the deepest roots.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (Indeed, in all of that are miraculous signs for people who apply their intellect) - For those who use their عَقْل (intellect), who think deeply, these aren't just observations about agriculture. This is about human nature, guidance and how different people respond to the same divine message.

Next time you look at the variation of plants in a garden, think about the variations among people and their personalities. Consider how differently people respond to the same message of truth.

translation: And if you are astonished, [O Muhammad] - then astonishing is their saying, "When we are dust, will we indeed be [brought] into a new creation?" Those are the ones who have disbelieved in their Lord, and those will have shackles upon their necks, and those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
وَإِن تَعْجَبْ فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ أَءِذَا كُنَّا تُرَٰبًا أَءِنَّا لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلُ فِىٓ أَعْنَاقِهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

وَإِن تَعْجَبْ فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ (And if you are in shock, then what is truly shocking is their statement) - Allah ﷻ is addressing the Prophet ﷺ. If you're shocked that they don't believe after all these clear signs, your shock is understandable. But what's genuinely strange, what's truly bizarre, is what they're saying.

أَءِذَا كُنَّا تُرَٰبًا أَءِنَّا لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (When we become dirt, will we really be brought back in a new creation?) - This is what shocks them about the message. They find resurrection unbelievable.

But think about what Allah ﷻ just described: He brings plants out of the earth. Seeds buried in dirt transform into living, growing, fruit-bearing plants. They witness this cycle constantly. How can they be shocked that human life will emerge from dirt? That's what's truly strange - they can't make this simple connection. Their inability to reflect on what they see every day is what's shocking.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ (Those are the ones who have disbelieved in their Master) - Allah ﷻ identifies them clearly. Their rejection of resurrection is ultimately a rejection of their Rabb, the One who created them and can certainly recreate them.

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلُ فِىٓ أَعْنَاقِهِمْ (And those are the ones who will have chains around their necks) - The word أَغْلَـٰلُ refers to chains, but specifically chains that bind multiple people together. One chain goes around a person's neck, connects to the next person's neck, then to the next. When one is pulled, they're all yanked together.

Who are you chained to? You're chained to the people you helped misguide, your partners in disbelief and wrongdoing. You're also chained to family members you had a responsibility toward but abandoned - those you never made dua for, those you didn't care enough about to have uncomfortable conversations with about faith.

Many of us have family members far from the deen. We avoid talking to them because it's uncomfortable. But like it or not, Allah ﷻ will chain us to them on Judgment Day. Whether we want those difficult conversations or not isn't the question. The question is only when and how we'll have them - because we will have to face those cousins, uncles and relatives eventually.

Adding to this graphic description: they have metal around their necks and they're in fire. The metal itself is burning against their skin.

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (Those are the companions of the Fire, in which they will remain eternally) - Their fate is sealed. They chose to reject clear signs, and now they face the consequences of that choice.

translation: They impatiently urge you to bring about evil before good, while there has already occurred before them similar punishments [to what they demand]. And indeed, your Lord is full of forgiveness for the people despite their wrongdoing, and indeed, your Lord is severe in penalty
commentary:
وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ وَقَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِمُ ٱلْمَثُلَـٰتُ ۗ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ لِّلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ ظُلْمِهِمْ ۖ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَشَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ (They rush you to bring the evil before the good) - The disbelievers aren't asking for good things from Allah ﷻ. They're not seeking His mercy or His blessings. Instead, they mockingly demand punishment. "Show us this punishment you keep warning us about!" They've become so hardened that they challenge the Messenger ﷺ to bring divine retribution upon them.

وَقَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِمُ ٱلْمَثُلَـٰتُ (Yet exemplary punishments have already passed before them) - The word مَثُلَـٰتُ comes from the root meaning "example" or "likeness." But مَثُلَـٰتُ specifically refers to punishments that serve as deterring examples for others - like when someone is made an example of to warn everyone else.

The verb form also carries the meaning of an image appearing in the mind. When Allah ﷻ says مَثُلَـٰتُ have been given to them, it means these people have heard the stories of previous nations. When you mention the Thamud or other destroyed peoples, a slideshow appears in their minds. They can visualize the ruins of those civilizations and their destruction. They have the historical evidence right in front of them.

وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ لِّلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ ظُلْمِهِمْ (And indeed, your Master possesses vast forgiveness for people despite their wrongdoing) - This is remarkable. They keep demanding punishment, yet Allah ﷻ is still offering forgiveness. He hasn't closed the doors of His mercy. The fact that Allah ﷻ doesn't immediately show them a sign is itself a form of forgiveness and respite.

We learned this lesson from the story of Ibrahim (AS). When angels came to deliver punishment, there was no turning back. Once they appear, it's over. That's why Allah's ﷻ delay is actually His mercy in action.

وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَشَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (Yet your Master is also severe in delivering consequences) - Don't confuse Allah's ﷻ forgiveness with weakness. Don't mistake His patience for inability to respond. When His punishment comes, it is devastating. He is شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - intense, severe, overwhelming in His retribution.

This ayah balances two aspects of Allah's ﷻ attributes: His infinite mercy that remains available even to those who mock Him, and His perfect justice that will inevitably be executed. Both are true. Both are real. Both should inform how we understand our relationship with Him.

translation: And those who disbelieved say, "Why has a sign not been sent down to him from his Lord?" You are only a warner, and for every people is a guide
commentary:
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُنذِرٌ ۖ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هَادٍ

The disbelievers persist in their demands, constantly saying: "Why hasn't a miracle come to him from his Master?" Their request is one they repeat again and again, as indicated by the present tense يَقُولُ - "they keep saying." But Allah ﷻ responds with a clear directive to His Messenger ﷺ: إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُنذِرٌ - "You are only a warner." 

This statement serves as both comfort and instruction. The Prophet ﷺ is being told not to concern himself with their demands for specific types of miracles. His role is defined and focused: to warn people of the consequences of their choices. The request for miracles is not his to fulfill - that decision belongs entirely to Allah ﷻ.

Then comes the powerful statement: وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هَادٍ- "And for every nation is a guide." The word هَادٍ is grammatically feminine in its ending, which adds a layer of meaning. This isn't just referring to messengers sent to nations, but to the various forms of guidance Allah ﷻ provides. Some nations received miracles they could see, others received miracles they could hear. The Quraysh are receiving the Quran itself as their miracle.

Allah ﷻ decides what form of guidance each nation receives. The distribution of these divine resources is His decision alone, not subject to human demands or preferences. The Quran itself affirms this principle when it says that guidance (هدى) comes in different forms for different peoples. Your only job, the ayah tells the Prophet ﷺ, is to keep warning.

Notice also that this surah emphasizes warning over glad tidings. You don't see وَبَشِّر ("and give good news") here. Why? Because the entire surah carries the tone of thunder. When you hear thunder, you don't hear good news - you hear a warning, something that keeps people awake at night, alert to approaching danger.

translation: Allah knows what every female carries and what the wombs lose [prematurely] or exceed. And everything with Him is by due measure
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنثَىٰ وَمَا تَغِيضُ ٱلْأَرْحَامُ وَمَا تَزْدَادُ ۖ وَكُلُّ شَىْءٍ عِندَهُۥ بِمِقْدَارٍ

The disbelievers are asking for punishment, challenging Allah's authority. But Allah ﷻ responds: Do you really think I don't know what you're up to? I knew about you before you even knew about yourself.

Allah ﷻ knows what every female carries - and this knowledge exists even before she herself knows she's expecting. She goes about her day completely unaware that new life is forming within her, but Allah ﷻ knows. And this isn't limited to human females - the ayah says كُلُّ أُنثَىٰ, every female of every species.

The ayah continues: وَمَا تَغِيضُ ٱلْأَرْحَامُ- "and what the wombs absorb." The word تَغِيضُ means to take in or absorb, like a seed being planted in soil. We've seen this word before in the Quran when water was absorbed into the earth. The womb takes in and absorbs what has been placed within it.

وَمَا تَزْدَادُ- "and what they increase." As the womb begins to expand and grow, Allah ﷻ knows every stage of that development. Every contraction, every expansion, every minute detail of growth within the mother's womb - all of it is known to Him.

The ayah concludes: وَكُلُّ شَىْءٍ عِندَهُۥ بِمِقْدَارٍ- "And everything with Him is by precise measure." Do you think you're getting away with anything? Allah ﷻ keeps precise calculation of everything, even what was happening inside your mother's womb when it contracted and when it expanded. If He has that level of control over you from before your birth, how can you imagine He doesn't have control over you now?

translation: [He is] Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Grand, the Exalted
commentary:
عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ٱلْكَبِيرُ ٱلْمُتَعَالِ

Allah ﷻ is عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ - the Knower of the unseen and the seen. He knows what is hidden from our perception and what is visible before our eyes.

Then come two names that create a powerful contrast: ٱلْكَبِيرُ- the Great, and ٱلْمُتَعَالِ- the Possessor of the Highest Heights. Think about the contrast being established here. The ayah just described how Allah ﷻ knows about the tiny life forming in the womb, something so small and hidden. Now He's described as Al-Kabeer - the Great One. This is the contrast between how small you were inside your mother and how Great He is.

Al-Muta'al, the Possessor of All Heights, connects to the elevation theme that runs throughout this surah. From the very beginning, we've been reading about mountains, about skies raised without pillars (رَفَعَ), about elevation. Allah ﷻ is the ultimate source of all height and elevation. When we say "Allahu Akbar" or make dua saying "Ya Allah," we're acknowledging His exalted position. The word مُتَعَالِ comes from عُلُوّ - the highest heights, far above anything in creation.

translation: It is the same [to Him] concerning you whether one conceals [his] speech or one publicizes it and whether one is hidden by night or conspicuous [among others] by day
commentary:
سَوَآءٌ مِّنكُم مَّنْ أَسَرَّ ٱلْقَوْلَ وَمَن جَهَرَ بِهِۦ وَمَنْ هُوَ مُسْتَخْفٍۭ بِٱلَّيْلِ وَسَارِبٌۢ بِٱلنَّهَارِ

The ayah begins with سَوَآءٌ - "The same" or "Equal." Before Allah ﷻ, it makes no difference whether you hide your speech or expose it, whether you whisper your plots in secret or announce them publicly. مَّنْ أَسَرَّ ٱلْقَوْلَ - whoever conceals speech, وَمَن جَهَرَ بِهِۦ - and whoever announces it openly - they're equal before His knowledge.

وَمَنْ هُوَ مُسْتَخْفٍۭ بِٱلَّيْلِ - "and whoever is hiding in the night." You might think darkness provides cover, that nighttime activities go unnoticed.

وَسَارِبٌۢ بِٱلنَّهَارِ - "and whoever moves openly by day." The word سَارِب is particularly interesting - it means to flow or move, and also refers to an animal's hole or den. So this encompasses both those who try to hide even during the day, ducking into hiding places, and those who move about openly. Some scholars interpret this as a reference to those who were actively plotting against the Prophet ﷺ, carrying out their schemes both in darkness and in daylight.

The point is clear: there is no hiding from Allah ﷻ. Neither the time of day nor your attempts at concealment make any difference. Nothing you say goes unnoticed, and there is nowhere you can be that is hidden from His sight.

translation: For each one are successive [angels] before and behind him who protect him by the decree of Allah. Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves. And when Allah intends for a people ill, there is no repelling it. And there is not for them besides Him any patron
commentary:
لَهُۥ مُعَقِّبَـٰتٌ مِّنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ يَحْفَظُونَهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُغَيِّرُ مَا بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا۟ مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ ۗ وَإِذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوٓءًا فَلَا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ ۚ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَالٍ

Given that Allah ﷻ knows everything and you cannot hide from Him, you might wonder: why hasn't He punished the evildoers yet? This ayah provides the answer.

لَهُۥ مُعَقِّبَـٰتٌ - "For him are successive guards." The word عَقَّبَ means to follow someone closely, persistently, like someone climbing a mountain and you're keeping up with them no matter how difficult the terrain. Picture a chase scene where someone tries every evasive maneuver - turning left, turning right, going between obstacles, switching directions - but the pursuer never lets go. That's the concept of اعقاب.

Allah ﷻ has assigned مُعَقِّبَـٰت - guardian angels - to every human being. The grammatical form tells us this is a small group, a minimal plural. These angels have a specific job: مِّنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ - "from in front of him and from behind him" - يَحْفَظُونَهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ - "they guard him by Allah's command."

Their protection is remarkable. If you're driving and your brakes should fail, they won't let them fail - not yet, because it's not your appointed time. They keep you safe until the moment Allah ﷻ has decreed. Some scholars also interpret this as referring specifically to the protection of the Messenger ﷺ - he has a divine security detail, angels stationed in front and behind, guarding him by Allah's command. The disbelievers plotting against him need not concern him.

But then the focus shifts back to the disbelievers themselves: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُغَيِّرُ مَا بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا۟ مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ - "Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is within themselves."

Don't worry about the Messenger's protection - worry about yourselves. Allah ﷻ will not bring punishment upon a nation until that nation has changed what is within themselves, until they have earned that punishment through their own choices and corruption.

وَإِذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوٓءًا فَلَا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ - "And when Allah intends harm for a people, there is no turning it back." Once a nation crosses that threshold, once Allah ﷻ has decreed that punishment will come, nothing can stop it. مَرَدَّ means to turn back or return - there's no canceling it, no reversing it.

وَمَا لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَالٍ - "And they have no protector besides Him." The word وَالٍ is more immediate than وَلِيّ. A wali might be a distant guardian, but a waal is someone standing right there to protect you at that very moment. When Allah's punishment comes, you won't find anyone - not even standing right beside you - who can protect you from it.

translation: It is He who shows you lightening, [causing] fear and aspiration, and generates the heavy clouds
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرِيكُمُ ٱلْبَرْقَ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا وَيُنشِئُ ٱلسَّحَابَ ٱلثِّقَالَ

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرِيكُمُ ٱلْبَرْقَ - "He is the One who shows you the lightning." Allah ﷻ now directs our attention to natural phenomena that carry both warning and mercy.

خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا - "fear and hope." Lightning inspires both emotions simultaneously. There's fear because lightning can be destructive, dangerous, a precursor to storms. But there's also hope - hope because if the land has been dry and you see lightning, you anticipate rain. In Surah Al-Baqarah, we read about travelers lost in darkness who see lightning and it shows them which direction to go. For them, the lightning becomes a source of hope, a guide in their confusion.

وَيُنشِئُ ٱلسَّحَابَ ٱلثِّقَالَ - "And He produces the heavy clouds." Why emphasize that the clouds are heavy? Light, wispy clouds don't carry lightning. Heavy clouds do. Heavy clouds also produce thunder, and heavy clouds are the ones that bring substantial rain. Those light, decorative clouds that look pleasant in the sky - they don't bring the rain that people need. It's the heavy, dark, imposing clouds that carry the blessing of water.

By mentioning the weight of the clouds, Allah ﷻ is preparing us for what comes next. We've just seen lightning. What naturally follows lightning in heavy clouds? Thunder.

translation: And the thunder exalts [Allah] with praise of Him - and the angels [as well] from fear of Him - and He sends thunderbolts and strikes therewith whom He wills while they dispute about Allah; and He is severe in assault
commentary:
وَيُسَبِّحُ ٱلرَّعْدُ بِحَمْدِهِۦ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ مِنْ خِيفَتِهِۦ وَيُرْسِلُ ٱلصَّوَٰعِقَ فَيُصِيبُ بِهَا مَن يَشَآءُ وَهُمْ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِى ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْمِحَالِ

وَيُسَبِّحُ ٱلرَّعْدُ بِحَمْدِهِۦ - "And the thunder declares His perfection with His praise." This is a stunning statement. Thunder itself - that powerful, earth-shaking sound - is engaged in tasbeeh, in declaring Allah's perfection. The thunder is doing SubhanAllah.

This means that when we hear thunder, SubhanAllah should naturally come to our tongues. Some narrations suggest we should say "Subhanar-raddi wal-hamd" - "Perfect is the Thunder and all praise belongs to Allah."

وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ مِنْ خِيفَتِهِۦ - "and the angels, out of fear of Him." The angels join the thunder in this tasbeeh, but their motivation is explicitly stated: fear. Fear of Allah ﷻ. Think about this - the thunder is scary to us, but the thunder and the angels are both in a state of fear before Allah ﷻ.

وَيُرْسِلُ ٱلصَّوَٰعِقَ - "And He sends thunderbolts" - فَيُصِيبُ بِهَا مَن يَشَآءُ - "and strikes with them whom He wills."

وَهُمْ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِى ٱللَّهِ - "while they argue about Allah." This is the striking contrast. All of this is happening in the sky - thunder declaring tasbeeh, lightning being prepared, angels trembling in fear of their Lord. And down on the ground, these tiny, insect-like human beings are standing there arguing about Allah ﷻ, debating His existence, questioning His authority. Do you realize the absurdity of what you're doing?

وَهُوَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْمِحَالِ - "And He is severe in strategy." This final phrase introduces a new word: مِحَال. This word combines multiple meanings. حَال means power and strength, while مَكْر means plotting and planning. محال brings them together - it's the power to execute a thoroughly planned attack against an enemy, the capability to completely overwhelm an opponent with both superior force and superior strategy. Allah ﷻ possesses both the power and the perfect plan to deal with those who oppose Him.

translation: To Him [alone] is the supplication of truth. And those they call upon besides Him do not respond to them with a thing, except as one who stretches his hands toward water [from afar, calling it] to reach his mouth, but it will not reach it [thus]. And the supplication of the disbelievers is not but in error [i.e. futility]
commentary:
لَهُۥ دَعْوَةُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَسْتَجِيبُونَ لَهُم بِشَىْءٍ إِلَّا كَبَـٰسِطِ كَفَّيْهِ إِلَى ٱلْمَآءِ لِيَبْلُغَ فَاهُ وَمَا هُوَ بِبَـٰلِغِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا دُعَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ

لَهُۥ دَعْوَةُ ٱلْحَقِّ - "To Him belongs the call of truth." This beautiful, concise phrase carries two interconnected meanings.

First: He alone possesses the invitation to truth. Islam is the call to truth, and it belongs entirely to Allah ﷻ. Even the Messenger ﷺ doesn't own this call - he cannot change or modify it according to his own will. The thunder does tasbeeh, the angels tremble in fear, and the Messenger ﷺ speaks only what Allah ﷻ reveals to him. The call belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

Second: The word دَعْوَة also means supplication, calling upon someone. So this means: He is the only One truly worthy of being called upon. All those people making dua - holding their hands in various positions according to their different religions and traditions - only those calling upon Allah ﷻ are making a real call. The rest are like dialing wrong numbers.

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَسْتَجِيبُونَ لَهُم بِشَىْءٍ - "And those they call upon besides Him do not respond to them in anything." These false gods, these idols, these intercessors people turn to - they cannot respond. They have no capacity to answer whatsoever.

إِلَّا كَبَـٰسِطِ كَفَّيْهِ إِلَى ٱلْمَآءِ لِيَبْلُغَ فَاهُ وَمَا هُوَ بِبَـٰلِغِهِۦ - "Except like one who extends his hands toward water to reach his mouth, but it will never reach him."

Picture this scene: a traveler desperate in the desert. He sees what he thinks is water - actually a mirage, a سَرَاب. In his desperation and delusion, he extends his hands toward it, cupping them as if to drink. But there's no water there. It will never reach his mouth because it doesn't exist. This is the image of someone making dua to other than Allah ﷻ - a delusional act, reaching for something that cannot possibly respond, grasping at an illusion.

وَمَا دُعَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ - "And the supplication of the disbelievers is nothing but in error." Their prayers, their invocations, their moments of worship - all of it is lost, wandering in misguidance, going nowhere.

translation: And to Allah prostrates whoever is within the heavens and the earth, willingly or by compulsion, and their shadows [as well] in the mornings and the afternoons
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ طَوْعًا وَكَرْهًا وَظِلَـٰلُهُم بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ ۩

وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ  - "And to Allah prostrates whoever is in the heavens and the earth." Notice that Allah ﷻ mentions the high before the low - the heavens before the earth. This connects to the recurring theme of elevation throughout this surah. The act of sujood itself is an act of coming low, of humility, and it's mentioned after all the discussion of height and elevation.

Everything in the skies and everything on earth makes sujood to Allah ﷻ. This is the reality of creation.

طَوْعًا وَكَرْهًا - "willingly and unwillingly." Some creation makes sujood by choice, with full consciousness and devotion. Others make sujood because they have no choice - it's built into their very nature. The stars, the planets, the trees, the mountains - they're in a constant state of submission to Allah's will, though not with conscious intention like believers.

وَظِلَـٰلُهُم بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ - "and their shadows in the morning and the afternoon."

This is a profound statement. Even shadows make sujood to Allah ﷻ. Some scholars interpret this to mean that when you see any shadow - the shadow of a tree, the shadow of a building, your own shadow - it's a reminder of sujood. The shadow itself is in a state of submission.

Every tree casts a shadow, every mountain casts a shadow, every planet casts a shadow. These shadows, shifting from morning (الْغُدُوِّ) to afternoon (الْـَٔاصَالِ), are engaged in their own form of sujood. Creation is in constant worship, even in ways we don't perceive. The entire universe is in a state of submission to its Creator.

This ayah is an ayah of sujood (۩), so when we recite it or hear it, we should prostrate, joining all of creation in this universal act of worship.

translation: Say, "Who is Lord of the heavens and earth?" Say, "Allah." Say, "Have you then taken besides Him allies not possessing [even] for themselves any benefit or any harm?" Say, "Is the blind equivalent to the seeing? Or is darkness equivalent to light? Or have they attributed to Allah partners who created like His creation so that the creation [of each] seemed similar to them?" Say, "Allah is the Creator of all things, and He is the One, the Prevailing
commentary:
قُلْ مَن رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلْ أَفَٱتَّخَذْتُم مِّن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا ۚ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ أَمْ هَلْ تَسْتَوِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتُ وَٱلنُّورُ ۗ أَمْ جَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ خَلَقُوا۟ كَخَلْقِهِۦ فَتَشَـٰبَهَ ٱلْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّـٰرُ

This ayah presents a powerful series of rhetorical questions designed to expose the illogic of shirk. Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ: "Say, 'Who is the Lord, the Master of the skies and the earth?'" The answer comes immediately: "Say, 'Allah.'" Then the challenge intensifies: "Have you dared to take besides Him protective friends and guardians who can't even have any control over their own selves in terms of benefit or harm?"

The ayah establishes a fundamental contrast. Allah ﷻ asks: "Is the one who can see and the one who can't see equal?" The blind person cannot appreciate what comes from above, cannot understand the majesty of the sky or recognize when something descends from the heavens. Similarly, "Are the shades of darkness and light ever going to be equal?"

Then comes a penetrating question about the mushrikeen's partners: "Have those who associated partners with Allah created like He creates, so that creation became confusing for them when they looked at it?" Did their so-called gods make something so similar to Allah's creation that people genuinely couldn't tell the difference? Of course not.

The ayah concludes with absolute clarity: "Say, 'Allah is the Creator of all things, and He is the One, the Undeniable, the Dominant.'" The name  القهار emphasizes Allah's complete dominance over all creation.

translation: He sends down from the sky, rain, and valleys flow according to their capacity, and the torrent carries a rising foam. And from that [ore] which they heat in the fire, desiring adornments and utensils, is a foam like it. Thus Allah presents [the example of] truth and falsehood. As for the foam, it vanishes, [being] cast off; but as for that which benefits the people, it remains on the earth. Thus does Allah present examples
commentary:
أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌۢ بِقَدَرِهَا فَٱحْتَمَلَ ٱلسَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا ۚ وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلنَّارِ ٱبْتِغَآءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَـٰعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَٱلْبَـٰطِلَ ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَآءً ۖ وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ ٱلنَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ

This ayah contains one of the most powerful parables in the Quran. Allah ﷻ describes how He sends water down from the sky, and valleys fill to their measure. The word زبد means foam, and رابيا comes from the root meaning "to rise." The floodwaters start carrying foam that rises to the surface.

Then Allah ﷻ presents a parallel scene: metals being burned in fire to make jewelry or utensils. As these metals are heated, similar foam rises to the top—impurities that must be separated from the pure metal.

Two types of foam are described here. The first comes from coolness—water from the sky producing foam in the valleys. The second comes from heat—burning metals to extract their purity. Notice that the water foam rises easily, but the metal impurities only rise when subjected to extreme temperatures.

This parable captures the entire seerah in a few verses. Water throughout this surah parallels revelation, the Quran. Just as water descended from the sky and filled every valley according to its capacity, the message of Islam spread through every street of Makkah. Some valleys are deep and can hold more water; others are shallow. Similarly, people received the message according to their capacity.

Water gives life to the earth, but it also reveals what is merely dirt and impurities on the surface. As the floodwaters rise, they carry away the debris—twigs, pebbles, dead leaves, and dust. The believers absorbed the water of revelation; the disbelievers rose to the top like foam, clearly distinguishable. There was no confusion about who would accept the message and who would reject it.

The second type of foam represents a different dynamic. When the Prophet ﷺ eventually migrated to Medina, a new challenge emerged: the hypocrites. Unlike the open disbelievers of Makkah who were easily identified (like water foam), the hypocrites were deeply embedded within the Muslim community. They required the heat of difficult circumstances to be exposed—the heat of battle at Badr, Uhud, and Al-Ahzab. Only under extreme pressure would their true nature rise to the surface.

Notice the two pursuits mentioned when burning metals: jewelry (حلية) or utensils (متاع). Jewelry is more valuable. This parallels the Muslim going into battle with two legitimate goals before them: the ultimate reward of Jannah (the jewelry) and the immediate victory and spoils of war (the utensils). Both are permissible, but priorities must remain clear. Keep the jewelry as jewelry and the utensils as utensils.

Allah ﷻ concludes: "That is how Allah strikes truth against falsehood." In the age of dawah, truth clashes with falsehood through the message spreading everywhere. In an established Muslim community, extremely difficult circumstances expose the hypocrites. The clash continues in different forms.

Then comes the beautiful conclusion about the foam: فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَآءً—"it will be cast out." The word جفاء refers to how a boiling kettle kicks out its bubbles, tossing them away like trash. The foam—representing both the open disbelievers and hidden hypocrites—will ultimately be eliminated.

"But as for what benefits people, it remains in the earth." The word يَمْكُثُ is profound. It doesn't just mean "to remain" but "to remain while waiting." It's related to the word مكث, meaning to stay somewhere in anticipation of something else. Even the believers who remain on earth don't consider it their permanent home—they're waiting for something better. They're not permanent residents; they're just waiting.

The ayah ends: كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ—"That is how Allah strikes examples." This brief parable captured the entire prophetic mission—the spread of the message, the separation of believers from disbelievers, the eventual exposure of hypocrites, and the ultimate triumph of truth. After presenting such eloquence and power, Allah ﷻ says, in effect, "That is how it's done."

translation: For those who have responded to their Lord is the best [reward], but those who did not respond to Him - if they had all that is in the earth entirely and the like of it with it, they would [attempt to] ransom themselves thereby. Those will have the worst account, and their refuge is Hell, and wretched is the resting place
commentary:
لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُۥ لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَمِثْلَهُۥ مَعَهُۥ لَٱفْتَدَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلْحِسَابِ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ

For those who responded to their Master is الحسنى —the very best, the most beautiful outcome. The previous ayah already established that those who didn't respond to Allah ﷻ are like foam, destined to be cast away. They have no real place on earth. But Allah ﷻ gives them the best case scenario, even though it's not their reality.

The word لو is critical here—it's a particle of regret, looking back at the past with the impossibility of change. Even if those who didn't respond had everything on the earth altogether, and then another earth's worth of wealth along with it, they would offer it all as ransom to escape their fate.

Then comes one of the most frightening statements in the Quran: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلْحِسَابِ—"Those are the people who will have the worst kind of audit." Allah ﷻ doesn't say they'll have the worst punishment directly. He says they'll have the worst accounting, the toughest line-by-line analysis. The terrifying implication is that there's no difference between this detailed accounting and the punishment itself. The accounting is so severe, so meticulous, that it merges with the punishment.

Their final abode is Jahannam, described as بِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ —"what a terrible resting place." The word mihad refers to a cradle, the comforting embrace of a mother. Instead of comfort and safety, they find torment.

translation: Then is he who knows that what has been revealed to you from your Lord is the truth like one who is blind? They will only be reminded who are people of understanding
commentary:
۞ أَفَمَن يَعْلَمُ أَنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ٱلْحَقُّ كَمَنْ هُوَ أَعْمَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ

This ayah continues the contrast established earlier. Allah ﷻ asks: "Is the one who knows that what has been sent down to you from your Master is the truth like someone who is blind?"

The concept of "sending down" keeps directing our attention upward to the sky. Someone who recognizes that revelation came from above possesses a fundamental awareness that the blind person lacks. The blind cannot appreciate the greatness of the sky or understand something descending from the heavens. They only know something is here, but they don't know where it came from.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ—"The only people who will make an effort to remember are the people who possess sound minds." The phrase أولو الألباب refers to those with intellect, understanding, and the ability to reflect deeply. They are the ones who will benefit from the reminder.

translation: Those who fulfill the covenant of Allah and do not break the contract
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَنقُضُونَ ٱلْمِيثَـٰقَ

Allah ﷻ now describes the characteristics of those with sound minds. First, they are those who fulfill the promise made with Allah ﷻ and don't violate the covenant. The word ميثاق keeps appearing throughout this surah, emphasizing a contract or covenant where both parties are equally aware of the seriousness of the agreement. These believers understand the weight of their commitment to Allah ﷻ and honor it completely.

translation: And those who join that which Allah has ordered to be joined and fear their Lord and are afraid of the evil of [their] account
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ وَيَخَافُونَ سُوٓءَ ٱلْحِسَابِ

This phrase—"those who keep joined what Allah has commanded to keep joined"—is remarkably comprehensive. Certain phrases in the Quran capture the essence of the entire religion, and this is one of them.

What must be kept together? Our loyalty to the Book and the Messenger must never be separated. Our concern for this life and the Akhirah must remain connected. Our knowledge and our actions must go hand in hand. Family relations must be maintained, not severed. The deen and dunya, when properly understood, must be balanced together. Things that Allah ﷻ designed to be united must never be taken apart.

These believers also fear their Master with خشية—a fear born from awareness and reverence—and they fear سُوٓءَ ٱلْحِسَابِ, the worst kind of accounting. This echoes the description from ayah 18, reminding us of the severity that awaits those who fail.

translation: And those who are patient, seeking the countenance of their Lord, and establish prayer and spend from what We have provided for them secretly and publicly and prevent evil with good - those will have the good consequence of [this] home
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ

These believers show patience seeking the pleasure of their Master—literally, "seeking His face." They establish prayer consistently. They spend from what Allah ﷻ has provided them, both secretly and publicly. This balanced approach to charity reflects wisdom: sometimes giving privately protects both the giver's sincerity and the recipient's dignity, while sometimes giving publicly encourages others and normalizes generosity.

Then comes a beautiful phrase: وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ. The word يدرءون comes from دفع, meaning to push something away forcefully—like pushing someone out of the path of an oncoming car. You might hurt them slightly in the push, but you save their life. These believers push away bad deeds by immediately following them with good deeds. They save themselves from the consequences of sins by rushing to perform righteous acts.

For these people: لَهُمْ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ—they will have the ultimate outcome of the home. The word عقبى is the feminine form of the superlative, referring to the absolute best possible final abode.

translation: Gardens of perpetual residence; they will enter them with whoever were righteous among their fathers, their spouses and their descendants. And the angels will enter upon them from every gate, [saying]
commentary:
جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن كُلِّ بَابٍ

Gardens of Eden—جَنَّـٰتُ is plural, indicating multiple gardens they will enter. How does one enter multiple gardens? Perhaps the entrance is from above, revealing multiple valleys extending into different gardens, each one available to explore.

Then Allah ﷻ mentions something extraordinary: وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ—"and whoever was righteous among their parents, their spouses, and their descendants."

This teaches us about the ranks of Jannah. There are four primary categories: the صالحين (righteous), the شهداء (martyrs), the صديقين (the truthful), and the نبيين (the prophets). The minimum passing grade is being among the righteous. Above them are the martyrs, then the truthful, and finally the rank that only those chosen by Allah ﷻ from the beginning can reach.

We know that the distance between levels of Jannah is vast—the Prophet ﷺ described it as the distance between earth and sky, or between you and the stars. Now imagine your cousin made it to a high level while you're at a lower level, or vice versa. This ayah teaches that if a family member reaches a higher level, they won't be brought down—instead, those at lower levels will be elevated to join them. Families can be reunited at the highest level any member achieved.

This isn't about people being dragged out of Hell because they have connections. The ayah specifically says "whoever was righteous"—everyone mentioned here already earned their place in Jannah. This is about the additional blessing of being elevated within Jannah to join family members who reached higher stations.

Perhaps you and your family barely made it into Jannah, but your great-great-grandfather is among the elevated ones. He can call you up. Or perhaps your ancestry didn't hold firmly to the deen, but future generations—your great-great-grandchildren—became champions of Islam and reached high ranks. They can bring up their ancestors who also made it to Jannah.

This can include parents, spouses, and descendants—multiple generations coming together. The angels enter from every door as escorts, continuously bringing family members: "We have your grandfather here, your uncle, your cousins." The scene is one of endless reunion and joy.

translation: Peace be upon you for what you patiently endured. And excellent is the final home
commentary:
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُم بِمَا صَبَرْتُمْ ۚ فَنِعْمَ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ

سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُم—"Peace be upon you." This greeting could come from multiple sources: from Allah ﷻ Himself, from the angels, from other believers, from all the family members being reunited. The ayah doesn't limit it, suggesting that peace surrounds them from every direction.

بِمَا صَبَرْتُمْ—"because of the patience you showed." This ties back to ayah 22, where patience seeking Allah's pleasure was mentioned. Everything they endured, every moment they remained steadfast, every time they held on—it all led to this.

فَنِعْمَ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ—"What an awesome final home!" The patience paid off in ways beyond imagination.

translation: But those who break the covenant of Allah after contracting it and sever that which Allah has ordered to be joined and spread corruption on earth - for them is the curse, and they will have the worst home
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مِيثَـٰقِهِۦ وَيَقْطَعُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ

Now Allah ﷻ describes the opposite group: those who violate the promise made with Allah ﷻ even after the covenant was established with mutual understanding. They cut apart what Allah ﷻ commanded to keep together—the exact opposite of the believers described in ayah 21.

Notice an important addition here: وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—"and they cause corruption in the land." In the earlier description of believers, their characteristics were largely internal—their relationship with Allah ﷻ, their prayer, their patience. But here, the corruption extends outward. When you violate the teachings of Islam, you're not just harming yourself. When you cut apart what Allah ﷻ commanded to keep together, there are always external consequences—damage to families, communities, and society.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱللَّعْنَةُ—"Those are the people who have the curse." وَلَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ—"And they will have the worst possible home."

The contrast is complete. The believers have عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ—the best ultimate home. These people have سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ—the worst home. The choice has been laid out with perfect clarity.

translation: Allah extends provision for whom He wills and restricts [it]. And they rejoice in the worldly life, while the worldly life is not, compared to the Hereafter, except [brief] enjoyment
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ وَفَرِحُوا۟ بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعٌ

Allah ﷻ expands the provision for whomever He wills, and He restricts it. The Arabic word يَبْسُطُ comes from بسط, which means to extend or open up, like someone extending their hands. It conveys the idea of abundance and capacity. The contrasting word يَقْدِرُ means He restricts or makes it precise—calculating exactly what someone deserves.

The ayah then shifts dramatically: "And they were overjoyed with the worldly life." The word فَرِحُوا describes a temporary state of joy, something fleeting that cannot be sustained permanently. This stands in stark contrast to what comes later in the passage about the tranquility that believers experience.

Allah ﷻ then asks: What is the worldly life in comparison to the Hereafter? The answer: nothing but متاع—temporary utility, mere interest. This is not the first time in Surah Ar-Ra'd that this comparison has been made. Earlier, Allah ﷻ described the rain that benefits people temporarily, while what benefits humanity remains in the earth. The same principle applies here: worldly pleasures are fleeting utilities, while the Hereafter holds true, lasting value.

translation: And those who disbelieved say, "Why has a sign not been sent down to him from his Lord?" Say, [O Muhammad], "Indeed, Allah leaves astray whom He wills and guides to Himself whoever turns back [to Him]
commentary:
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنَابَ

The disbelievers repeat their demand: "Why hasn't a miracle been sent down to him from his Master?" This is their constant refrain, their repeated objection. Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ to respond with a profound truth: "Indeed, Allah lets whoever He wills go astray, and He guides to Himself whoever returns to Him."

The word أَنَابَ is significant—it means to return, but specifically in a spiritual sense. This is one of the words for repentance and turning back to Allah ﷻ. The ayah suggests something critical: they're asking for a miracle, but Allah ﷻ is saying that if they had any genuine intention of returning to Him, the guidance already present would have been sufficient for them. The Quran itself would have been miracle enough. But because they have no real intention of turning back to Allah ﷻ, no miracle would satisfy them. The issue isn't the absence of signs; it's the absence of sincerity in their hearts.

translation: Those who have believed and whose hearts are assured by the remembrance of Allah. Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَتَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُم بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ أَلَا بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ ٱلْقُلُوبُ

This ayah describes those who truly return to Allah ﷻ: "Those who believe and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah." Notice the shift in vocabulary. When describing worldly joy earlier, Allah ﷻ used فَرِحُوا —temporary elation. But here, He uses تَطْمَئِنُّ, which means tranquility, calmness, serenity. The word طمأنينة describes a state of constancy, a permanent settling of the heart that can be maintained continuously. Unlike the fleeting happiness that comes from worldly things, the peace that comes from remembering Allah ﷻ can become a lasting condition.

Then comes the emphasis: أَلَا بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ ٱلْقُلُوبُ—"Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured." This is not just poetic language; it's a profound psychological and spiritual principle. The remembrance of Allah ﷻ—His dhikr—is itself a healer. It calms people down, brings down anxiety, soothes anger, relieves stress. For anyone studying Islamic counseling or psychology, this ayah provides a foundational therapeutic principle: the dhikr of Allah ﷻ calms hearts. It's not just an emotional experience; it affects all the senses, bringing comprehensive peace to the human being.

translation: Those who have believed and done righteous deeds - a good state is theirs and a good return
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ طُوبَىٰ لَهُمْ وَحُسْنُ مَـَٔابٍ

"Those who believed and did righteous deeds—for them is طُوبَىٰ and the most beautiful place of return."

The word طُوبَىٰ is remarkable. It describes a joy that brightens the heart and all the senses—not just an emotional feeling of happiness, but a comprehensive state of exhilaration that affects your entire being. It's the kind of joy that makes everything feel more alive.

Then comes حُسْنُ مَـَٔابٍ—the most beautiful place of return. The word مَآبٍ comes from the root أَوَبَ, meaning to return. This word is specifically used for living beings making a return journey. Think of traveling somewhere and then making your way back home—that return trip is your ma'ab.

There's a beautiful hint embedded here: human beings were originally taken out of Paradise, and now they finally get to come back. What a magnificent return journey—back to the original home that was made for humanity in the first place. This isn't just going somewhere new; it's returning to where we were always meant to be.

translation: Thus have We sent you to a community before which [other] communities have passed on so you might recite to them that which We revealed to you, while they disbelieve in the Most Merciful. Say, "He is my Lord; there is no deity except Him. Upon Him I rely, and to Him is my return
commentary:
كَذَٰلِكَ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ فِىٓ أُمَّةٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهَآ أُمَمٌ لِّتَتْلُوَا۟ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ قُلْ هُوَ رَبِّى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ مَتَابِ

"That is how We sent you into a nation that would come after many nations before it had passed away—so that you would recite to them what has been revealed to you. Yet they disbelieve in Ar-Rahman."

The specific mention of الرَّحْمَـٰنِ here carries multiple layers of meaning. First, many nations have come before, and their examples are being recited to these people. They're not witnessing anything unprecedented. These previous nations exhausted Allah's mercy—they kept pushing and pushing until His mercy ran out for them, and they were destroyed. The disbelievers of Makkah are now being shown this same pattern, yet they're rejecting الرَّحْمَـٰنِ—the Extremely Merciful. They're being shown that you can actually disqualify yourself from Allah's mercy. While الرَّحِيمِ describes Allah's constant, enduring mercy for the believers, الرَّحْمَـٰنِ describes His encompassing mercy that can be withdrawn if people persistently reject it.

Additionally, whenever the Prophet ﷺ would begin with بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ, the Quraysh would object: "Who is this Rahman? We've never heard of this name before. What are you talking about?" So they specifically disbelieved in Ar-Rahman as a name and attribute of Allah ﷻ.

Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ to respond simply: "Say: He is my Master. There is no one worthy of worship and obedience in any way, shape or form except Him. Upon Him alone I have relied and placed my trust, and to Him is my place of return." The word مَتَابِ carries both physical and spiritual meanings—it's both the place to return to and the act of repenting and turning back spiritually to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And if there was any qur'an by which the mountains would be removed or the earth would be broken apart or the dead would be made to speak, [it would be this Qur'an], but to Allah belongs the affair entirely. Then have those who believed not accepted that had Allah willed, He would have guided the people, all of them? And those who disbelieve do not cease to be struck, for what they have done, by calamity - or it will descend near their home - until there comes the promise of Allah. Indeed, Allah does not fail in [His] promise
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْءَانًا سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ ٱلْجِبَالُ أَوْ قُطِّعَتْ بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضُ أَوْ كُلِّمَ بِهِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۗ بَل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْرُ جَمِيعًا ۗ أَفَلَمْ يَا۟يْـَٔسِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن لَّوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ لَهَدَى ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا ۗ وَلَا يَزَالُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ تُصِيبُهُم بِمَا صَنَعُوا۟ قَارِعَةٌ أَوْ تَحُلُّ قَرِيبًا مِّن دَارِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ وَعْدُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ

"And if there was a Quran by which mountains would be moved, or the earth would be split apart, or the dead would be spoken to..."

Allah ﷻ doesn't even complete the sentence. It's left hanging with an implied "even then, they wouldn't believe." It's like saying "dot, dot, dot"—the conclusion is obvious. Instead, He immediately follows with: بَل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْرُ جَمِيعًا—"Rather, the entire matter rests with Allah." In other words, these disbelievers don't get to dictate what signs Allah ﷻ will send and what He won't send. That decision belongs entirely to Him. They're not in charge of revelation.

Then comes a powerful rhetorical question directed at the believers: "Have the believers not lost hope—have they not realized—that if Allah had willed, He would have guided all of humanity at once?" This addresses a deep concern believers might have: Why aren't more people accepting Islam? Why is the message being rejected? Allah ﷻ is telling them: Don't lose hope, but also understand that if Allah ﷻ had wanted, He could have guided everyone immediately. But that's not His plan. Don't entertain those thoughts of despair. Don't become frustrated with the slow progress.

The ayah continues: "And the disbelievers will continue to be afflicted by terrible calamity because of what they have done." The word قَارِعَةٌ refers to a loud, shocking, catastrophic event. The eventual consequences coming their way cannot be dispelled or canceled out, no matter what they do. "Or it will descend very close to their homes"—meaning some of these consequences will start happening near where they live. The people of Makkah began experiencing difficulties close to their territory, battles and conflicts at their doorstep. These are the beginning tremors before the full promise of Allah ﷻ arrives. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ—"Indeed, Allah does not go back on His promise."

translation: And already were [other] messengers ridiculed before you, and I extended the time of those who disbelieved; then I seized them, and how [terrible] was My penalty
commentary:
وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ

"Messengers before you were certainly ridiculed and made fun of. Then I extended time for those who disbelieved. Then I seized them. So how was My punishment?"

This ayah provides context for understanding Allah's response to mockery of His messengers. The word أَمْلَيْتُ means "I extended"—I gave them more rope, more time. Think of a dog on a leash. If it has a one-foot leash and runs wild, it gets choked immediately. But if you give it a 200-foot leash and it goes galloping at full speed, by the time it hits the end of that leash, the impact is devastating. It chokes itself much harder. This is the meaning of giving them extension—it's not mercy in the sense of forgiveness; it's allowing them to accumulate their own destruction.

Then comes أَخَذْتُهُمْ—"I seized them." Notice Allah ﷻ uses the past tense, as though it's already accomplished, already a done deal. This conveys certainty and inevitability. فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ—"So how was My punishment?" This is a rhetorical question. Look at history and see how those nations were destroyed. The answer is obvious: His punishment was devastating.

translation: Then is He who is a maintainer of every soul, [knowing] what it has earned, [like any other]? But to Allah they have attributed partners. Say, "Name them. Or do you inform Him of that which He knows not upon the earth or of what is apparent of speech?" Rather, their [own] plan has been made attractive to those who disbelieve, and they have been averted from the way. And whomever Allah leaves astray - there will be for him no guide
commentary:
أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قَآئِمٌ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ ۗ وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ قُلْ سَمُّوهُمْ ۚ أَمْ تُنَبِّـُٔونَهُۥ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَم بِظَـٰهِرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۗ بَلْ زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مَكْرُهُمْ وَصُدُّوا۟ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ

"As for the One who stands over every soul, watching over everything it has done..."

This sentence, like the previous conditional statement, is incomplete—it trails off deliberately. The implication is clear: Are they really going to reject and disbelieve in Someone who stands over every single soul, fully aware of everything each person has done?

"Yet they have made partners with Allah." The absurdity is staggering. Allah ﷻ then commands: قُلْ سَمُّوهُمْ—"Say: Name them!" This is a direct challenge. Go ahead, identify these gods. Who are they? Where did they come from? What are their histories? Tell us about them.

The rhetorical questions continue: "Or are you informing Him—meaning Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ—of something He doesn't know about in the land?" As if some secret gods emerged that Allah ﷻ wasn't aware of. "Or is it just outward speech?"—meaning empty words with no substance behind them. The implication here is important: when the Messenger ﷺ asks them to name their gods, they don't want to look foolish or be left speechless. So they start making things up, fabricating responses just to avoid appearing stumped, just to show they have something to say.

بَلْ زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مَكْرُهُمْ—"Rather, the fact is that their plotting and scheming has been beautified for them." Their own schemes look attractive to them; they've deceived themselves. وَصُدُّوا۟ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ—"And they were themselves obstructed from the path." They became barriers to their own guidance.

The ayah concludes: "And whoever Allah ﷻ lets go astray will find no guide whatsoever." This contrasts sharply with the beginning of the surah, where Allah ﷻ mentioned that when someone kicks away the gift that guidance was offering them, they have no guide left. Here, we see the consequence of persistent rejection: complete loss of guidance.

translation: For them will be punishment in the life of [this] world, and the punishment of the Hereafter is more severe. And they will not have from Allah any protector
commentary:
لَّهُمْ عَذَابٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَشَقُّ ۖ وَمَا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ

"They will have punishment in worldly life, and the punishment of the Hereafter is أَشَقُّ —far more difficult."

The word أَشَقُّ comes from شَقَّ, which describes something extremely difficult or hard. When something becomes شَاقّ, it's a task so difficult it's way beyond your capability to handle. The punishment of the Akhirah is beyond any creature's ability to endure. Nobody can handle it. Nobody will develop a tolerance for it. Any creature would die if they experienced it—except that Allah ﷻ has created those destined for Hell in such a way that they cannot die by definition. Though they cannot handle it, they cannot escape it through death either.

وَمَا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ—"And they will not have any protector from Allah's side whatsoever." No one will shield them, no one will defend them, no one will come between them and their punishment.

translation: The example of Paradise, which the righteous have been promised, is [that] beneath it rivers flow. Its fruit is lasting, and its shade. That is the consequence for the righteous, and the consequence for the disbelievers is the Fire
commentary:
مَّثَلُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۖ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ أُكُلُهَا دَآئِمٌ وَظِلُّهَا ۚ تِلْكَ عُقْبَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ ۖ وَّعُقْبَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ ٱلنَّارُ

"The example of the Garden that has been promised to those who protected themselves..."

What's significant about the word مَثَل—example or parable? It's something you can visualize. Allah ﷻ is telling us to try to imagine Paradise, to use the best of our capability to picture it. And here's the beautiful promise: whatever you imagine, it's going to be better than that. So when you recite these ayahs, when you memorize them, you're actually supposed to let your imagination go a little wild. It's encouraged. Try to visualize what's being described.

"Rivers flowing beneath it"—perhaps there are valleys, or hills upon hills of gardens, and at the foot of each, rivers are flowing. أُكُلُهَا دَآئِمٌ—"Its fruits are constant." They don't spoil, they don't go bad. They're always there, fresh and perfect, without any preservatives. وَظِلُّهَا—"And its shade is constant."

Think about those perfect moments you've experienced: a day at a picnic where the breeze is just right, where just enough sunlight comes through the leaves, where you're sitting with a beautiful view of water, and you think, "I wish every day could be like this. I wish this moment would never end. I wish it wouldn't get any hotter or cooler. This is perfect right now." That feeling of perfect contentment—that's what's being described, except it never ends. It stays that way forever.

We've all had fruits—apples, oranges, strawberries—but sometimes you bite into one and it's extraordinary. "Wow, where did you get these strawberries?" But then you eat the next one and it's not quite the same. In Paradise, أُكُلُهَا دَآئِمٌ—the taste, the texture, the quality is constant. Perfect every single time.

تِلْكَ عُقْبَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟—"That is the final home, the ultimate outcome of those who protected themselves." وَعُقْبَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ ٱلنَّارُ—"And the final home of the disbelievers is the Fire." That's the only description necessary for them. The contrast couldn't be starker.

translation: And [the believers among] those to whom We have given the [previous] Scripture rejoice at what has been revealed to you, [O Muhammad], but among the [opposing] factions are those who deny part of it. Say, "I have only been commanded to worship Allah and not associate [anything] with Him. To Him I invite, and to Him is my return
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ مَن يُنكِرُ بَعْضَهُۥ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَلَآ أُشْرِكَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا۟ وَإِلَيْهِ مَـَٔابِ

"And those to whom We gave the Scripture previously—they are overjoyed with what has been revealed to you."

These were the Jews and Christians who recognized that this man claiming to be a messenger was fulfilling and confirming previous scripture. They had heard the prophecies: "The final messenger is coming. The comforter is coming to deliver people God's words one final time, uncorrupted." They had been waiting for this to happen. So when it finally arrived, they didn't see it as competition or as a negation of their faith. They saw it as a confirmation of it. They were celebrating.

There's a beautiful story about one of the Companions (RA) whose ancestors had moved to Madinah generations earlier specifically because they believed, according to prophecy, that the final messenger would come there. As each generation died, they would write a letter: "O Messenger of Allah, we came waiting for you. Perhaps our children will see you." This Companion showed the Prophet ﷺ a letter from six or seven generations back, and he was overwhelmed with emotion: "I get to see what my ancestors didn't get to see." More than 600 years had passed since people of the Book began waiting, and now they finally witnessed it fulfilled.

وَمِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ مَن يُنكِرُ بَعْضَهُۥ—"And among the factions there are those who deny some of it." This is crucial: denying some of the Book means denying all of it. Allah ﷻ doesn't offer a discount program. It's not "pick and choose what you like." Yet people will come and say, "We appreciate the Quran. There are just some issues we have. But other than that, it's pretty decent. The Quran has some nice things to say."

We need to understand: when someone says "the Quran has some nice things to say," that's not a compliment we should accept gratefully. All the nice things are here. This is the word of your Master. Don't let anyone reduce it to having "some" nice things.

Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ to keep his message simple: إِنَّمَآ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَلَآ أُشْرِكَ بِهِۦٓ—"I have only been commanded to worship Allah and not associate anything with Him." This is significant because these scholars of Christianity and Judaism were well-educated, articulate people. When they spoke, they sounded scholarly and sophisticated. It would be easy to feel intellectually overwhelmed. But the Messenger ﷺ is told: Keep it simple. Don't try to impress them. Don't get caught up in philosophical explanations.

إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا۟—"To Him alone I call." وَإِلَيْهِ مَـَٔابِ—"And to Him is the final place of return." That's it. That's the entire message. We came from Him, and we're going back to Him. If you accept it, you accept it.

translation: And thus We have revealed it as an Arabic legislation. And if you should follow their inclinations after what has come to you of knowledge, you would not have against Allah any ally or any protector
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا عَرَبِيًّا ۚ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ مَا لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا وَاقٍ

"And that is how We have sent it down as an Arabic حُكْمًا—a decisive word, a word full of wisdom."

The word حُكْم carries the meaning of both wisdom and decisiveness. You don't have to explain Islam through elaborate philosophical frameworks that sound new and cutting edge. Just present the Quran. Allah ﷻ has given you what you need to present. The purpose of revelation is to speak to people directly. You don't have to speak on behalf of revelation—you can let revelation speak for itself.

Over time, a natural paradox has emerged. On one hand, the Quran and the Sunnah of our Messenger ﷺ have given birth to endless depths of scholarship. This is beautiful—there are layers upon layers of study that will never come to an end. On the other hand, this has created a tragedy: people feel they can no longer connect with revelation directly. They think they have to go through enormous scholarly hoops before they can understand Allah's word.

But think about it: the Quraysh had no idea what a messenger was, what the Akhirah was, yet they had a direct connection with the Quran. So our deen requires both things simultaneously. Yes, there's a road of deep learning—tafsir, Arabic grammar, fiqh, usul—that never comes to an end. You can spend your whole life on it and barely scratch the surface of Allah's Book. But introducing people to Allah's direct conversation—that's also part of our job. Allah ﷻ addresses people directly: يَا أَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ—"O people, listen up!" Why do we need to give a lengthy tafsir of "Ya ayyuhan-nas"? That itself is the speech. Give that to people. Share that with people.

This applies within Muslim families too. We've become so disconnected from the Quran. Parents should sit with their children and say, "You know what Allah ﷻ says to us here? Let me show you." When your child comes with a problem—"My friend was mean to me today"—open the Quran: "Let's see what Allah says about that. لَا يَسْخَرْ قَوْمٌ مِّن قَوْمٍ—people shouldn't make fun of other people. That's what Allah says." And when they ask, "But they did it to me," you respond: "You know what else Allah says? إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ—Allah is with those who are patient." Let Allah's word become part of your daily conversation.

حُكْمًا عَرَبِيًّا—"A decisive Arabic word." Perhaps this also addresses criticism from some People of the Book: "Why isn't it in the language of the other scriptures?" This final, decisive word is in Arabic because it was revealed to an Arab prophet for an Arab audience first, and from there to all of humanity.

وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ—"And if you were to follow their empty desires after knowledge has come to you..."

What knowledge? The Quran itself. This is the knowledge that has come to the Messenger ﷺ. The implication is profound: they might want you to talk about something other than the Quran. "This Quran is too direct, too blunt, too uncompromising. Allah talks to us like He owns us." Well, He does own us. Someone who owns you will talk to you in a certain way. Someone who works for you talks differently. Someone negotiating with you has a different tone. Someone trying to make a sale has to butter you up, compliment you: "Wow, nice car! Where'd you get that shirt?"

Allah ﷻ doesn't have to do any of that. He's very uncompromising. He tells it exactly as it is. He calls people out: مَا لَكُمْ—"What's wrong with you?"
